    Metallicity Gradients

    Metallicity Gradients

    Type:Observable / Diagnostic
    Level:Specialist
    Typical values:−0.01 to −0.1 dex/kpc (negative = decreasing outward)
    Related: Spatially resolved stellar populations
    Star formation
    Galaxy mergers
    Emission-line diagnostics

    Metallicity gradients describe the radial variation of chemical abundances — both stellar and gas-phase (nebular) — within galaxies. Most disk galaxies show negative gradients (higher metallicity in the center, lower in the outskirts), reflecting inside-out formation where the denser central regions formed stars earlier and enriched more rapidly[1][2].

    Stellar vs. Nebular Metallicity

    Stellar metallicity reflects the integrated chemical enrichment history, measured through absorption-line fitting of stellar populations[1]. Nebular (gas-phase) metallicity traces the current ISM enrichment level, measured through emission-line ratios using strong-line calibrations (e.g., O3N2, N2 methods)[3]. Both types of gradients carry diagnostic information about galaxy formation history, but they can differ — gas-phase metallicity responds more quickly to inflows and outflows.

    Inside-Out Formation Signatures

    Steep negative metallicity gradients are a hallmark of undisturbed inside-out disk formation: the central regions formed stars earlier, reaching higher metallicities through extended chemical enrichment[2][4]. The M101 group preserves these steep gradients, consistent with relatively pristine evolution[5].

    Flattening by Interactions

    Galaxy interactions can flatten metallicity gradients through radial gas mixing, interaction-triggered star formation, and redistribution of enriched material[5][3]. The M51 group shows flatter age–Σ★ and nebular metallicity–Σ★ relations compared to M101, interpreted as evidence of more advanced dynamical evolution driven by interactions[5]. This contrast provides direct observational evidence that group environment modulates chemical abundance gradients.

    See Also

    • Spatially resolved stellar populations
    • Emission-line diagnostics
    • Galaxy groups
    • Thainá-Batista et al. (2026) — Metallicity gradients in M51 vs M101

    References
    1. González Delgado, R. M. et al. (2015). "Spatially resolved stellar populations from CALIFA. II." A&A.
    2. Spitoni, E. et al. (2020). "Chemical evolution models and metallicity gradients." A&A. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/202037879
    3. Barrera-Ballesteros, J. K. et al. (2016). "Resolved metallicity relations in CALIFA galaxies." MNRAS. DOI:10.1093/mnras/stw1984
    4. Gallazzi, A. et al. (2005). "Ages and metallicities of galaxies in the local universe." MNRAS, 362, 41. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2005.09321.x
    5. Thainá-Batista, J. et al. (2026). "Spatially resolved stellar populations in the M51 group with J-PLUS. II." A&A (accepted). arXiv:2603.15869
